nearby_text: . In the general case, this requires numerical iteration to obtain the equilibrium heritability and genetic variance. However, in many cases, the phenotypic variance after selection can be written as $$ \sigma_{z^{*}}^{2}=\left(1-\kappa\right)\sigma_{z}^{2} $$ (16.10a) where $ \kappa $ is a constant independent of the current value of the variance. label: Formula 16.12c
section: Short-term Changes in the Variance: 1. Changes in the Additive Variance: Introduction
subsection: CHANGES IN VARIANCE UNDER TRUNCATION SELECTION
nearby_text: The within-generation change in the variance due to selection becomes $$ \delta(\sigma_{z(t)}^{2})=-\kappa\sigma_{z}^{2}(t)=-\kappa\frac{\sigma_{A}^{2}(t)}{h^{2}(t)} $$ (16.12c) where we have used the identity $ \sigma_z^2 = \sigma_A^2/h^2 $. Substituting Equation 16.12c into Equation 16.7a recovers the result of Bulmer (1974a), $$ d(t+1)=\frac{d(t)}{2}-\frac{\kappa}{2}h^{2}

label: Formula 16.12d

nearby_text: \frac{\sigma_{A}^{2}(t)}{h^{2}(t)} $$ (16.12c) where we have used the identity $ \sigma_z^2 = \sigma_A^2/h^2 $. Substituting Equation 16.12c into Equation 16.7a recovers the result of Bulmer (1974a), $$ d(t+1)=\frac{d(t)}{2}-\frac{\kappa}{2}h^{2}(t)\sigma_{A}^{2}(t)=\frac{d(t)}{2}-\frac{\kappa}{2}\frac{\left[\sigma_{a}^{2}+d(t)\right]^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}+d(t)} $$ (16.12d) with last step following from $ h^{2}\sigma_{A}^{2}=(\sigma_{A}^{2}/\sigma_{z}^{2})\sigma_{A}^{2}=\sigma_{A}^{4}/\sigma_{z}^{2} $

nearby_text: This quadratic equation in $ \tilde{d} $ has one admissible solution (the constraint being that $ \tilde{\sigma}_A^2 = \tilde{d} + \sigma_a^2 \geq 0 $). Solving yields $$ \widetilde{\sigma}_{A}^{2}=\sigma_{z}^{2}\gamma,\quad\mathrm{w h e r e}\quad\gamma=\frac{2h^{2}-1+\sqrt{1+4h^{2}(1-h^{2})\kappa}}{2(1+\kappa)} $$ (16.13a) Because $ \widetilde{\sigma}_{A}^{2}-\sigma_{A}^{2}=\widetilde{d} $, we can write $$ \begin{align*}\widetilde{\sigma}^2_z=\sigma^2_z+(\widetilde{\sigma}^2_A-\sigma^2_A)=\sigma^2_z(1+\gamma-h^2)\end{a

If selection reduces the variance in z ( $ 0 < \\kappa < 1 $), then the variance in any correlated character is also reduced,"
}

label: Formula 16.14c
section: CHANGES IN VARIANCE UNDER TRUNCATION SELECTION
subsection: Changes in Correlated Characters
nearby_text: ted character is also reduced, independent of the sign of the correlation (as change is a function of $ \rho^{2} $). Likewise, the covariance between x and y following selection on z is calculated by $$ \sigma(x^{*},y^{*})=\sigma(x,y)-\kappa\frac{\sigma(x,z)\sigma(y,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}} $$ (16.14c) yielding a within-generation change in this covariance of $$ \delta\left[\sigma(x,y)\right]=-\kappa\frac{\sigma(x,z)\sigma(y,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}} $$ (16.14d)

      {
        "id": "formula_16.5b",

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\sigma^{2}(z_{o})&=\\frac{h^{4}}{4}\\left[\\sigma^{2}(z_{m}^{*})+\\sigma^{2}(z_{f}^{*})\\right]+\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\\\&=\\frac{h^{4}}{2}\\left[\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\delta(\\sigma_{z}^{2})\\right]+\\left(1-\\frac{h^{4}}{2}\\right)\\sigma_{z}^{2}\\\\&=\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\frac{h^{4}}{2}\\delta\\left(\\sigma_{z}^{2}\\right)\\\\ \\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula 16.6",
        "section": "Short-term Changes in the Variance: 1. Changes in the Additive Variance: Introduction",

        "label": "Formula 16.8b",

nearby_text: ppa > 0 $), generating negative values of $ d $ and a corresponding reduction in both the additive variance and heritability.   "nearby_text": "ppa > 0 $), generating negative values of $ d $ and a corresponding reduction in both the additive variance and heritability. When the trait is normally distributed, recalling LW Equation 2.15 yields $$ \\sigma_{z^{*}}^{2}=\\left[1-\\bar{\\imath}\\left(\\bar{\\imath}-x_{[1-p]}\\right)\\right]\\sigma_{z}^{2} $$ (16.15a) and hence, as given in Table 16.1, $$ \\kappa=\\overline{\\imath}\\left(\\overline{\\imath}-x_{[1-p]}\\right) $$ (16.15b) where $ \\bar{\\imath} $ is the selection intensity (Equation 14.3)."
}

label: Formula 16.18a
section: Short-term Changes in the Variance: 1. Changes in the Additive Variance: Introduction
subsection: RESPONSE UNDER NORMALIZING SELECTION
nearby_text: otypes are normally distributed before selection, with a mean of $ \mu $ and variance of $ \sigma_{z}^{2} $, then after selection, phenotypes remain normally distributed, with a new mean and variance $$ \mu^{*}=\mu+\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}(\theta-\mu)\quad and\quad\sigma_{z^{*}}^{2}=\sigma_{z}^{2}\left(1-\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}\right) $$ (16.18a)

label: Formula 16.18b
section: Short-term Changes in the Variance: 1. Changes in the Additive Variance: Introduction
subsection: RESPONSE UNDER NORMALIZING SELECTION
nearby_text: essions remain linear throughout. Thus, we can apply the breeder's equation to predict changes in the mean and Equation 16.7a to predict changes in the variance (under the infinitesimal model). Here, $$ S=\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}}(\theta-\mu)\quad and\quad\delta\left(\sigma_{z^{*}}^{2}\right)=-\frac{\sigma_{z}^{4}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\omega^{2}} $$ (16.18b)

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\sigma_{A}^{2}(t+1)&=\\sigma_{A_{o}}^{2}(t+1)=\\sigma^{2}\\left(\\frac{A_{m}(t)}{2}+\\frac{A_{f}(t)}{2}\\right)+\\sigma_{e}^{2}\\\\&=\\frac{1}{4}\\left(\\sigma_{A_{m}}^{2}(t)+\\sigma_{A_{f}}^{2}(t)\\right)+\\frac{1}{2}\\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)\\\\&=\\frac{1}{2}\\sigma_{A^{*}}^{2}(t)+\\frac{1}{2}\\sigma_{a}^{2}\\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula 16.8b",
        "section": "CHANGES IN VARIANCE UNDER THE INFINITESIMAL MODEL",
        "subsection": "Within- and Among-Family Variance Under the Infinitesimal Model",
        "position": 18,
        "symbols_defined": [

        ]

        "latex": "\\Delta d(t)=-\\frac{d(t)}{2}\\left(1+\\frac{1}{N_{e}}\\right)-\\frac{1}{2}\\left(1-\\frac{1}{N_{e}}\\right)\\kappa h^{2}(t)\\sigma_{A}^{2}(t)",

        "label": "Formula 16.18a",
        "section": "Short-term Changes in the Variance: 1. Changes in the Additive Variance: Introduction",

  "nearby_text": "essions remain linear throughout. Thus, we can apply the breeder's equation to predict changes in the mean and Equation 16.7a to predict changes in the variance (under the infinitesimal model). Here, $$ S=\\frac{\\sigma_{z}^{2}}{\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\omega^{2}}(\\theta-\\mu)\\quad and\\quad\\delta\\left(\\sigma_{z^{*}}^{2}\\right)=-\\frac{\\sigma_{z}^{4}}{\\sigma_{z}^{2}+\\omega^{2}} $$ (16.18b)"
}

label: Formula 16.19
section: Short-term Changes in the Variance: 1. Changes in the Additive Variance: Introduction
subsection: RESPONSE UNDER NORMALIZING SELECTION
nearby_text: abilizing selection can occur simultaneously with normalizing selection, as when $ \mu \neq \theta $, the mean changes under selection. Substituting Equation 16.18b into the breeder's equation yields $$ R(t)=h^{2}(t)S(t)=h^{2}(t)\frac{\sigma_{z}^{2}(t)\left[\theta-\mu(t)\right]}{\sigma_{z}^{2}(t)+\omega^{2}} $$ (16.19)

section: Short-term Changes in the Variance: 1. Changes in the Additive Variance: Introduction

nearby_text: above it. However, Equation 16.18a shows that the change in variance is independent of the current mean value, $ \mu $. From Equation 16.7a, the change in the disequilibrium contribution is given by $$ d(t+1)=\frac{d(t)}{2}-\frac{h^{4}(t)}{2}\frac{\sigma_{z}^{4}(t)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}(t)+\omega^{2}}=\frac{d(t)}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\frac{[\sigma_{a}^{2}+d(t)]^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}+d(t)+\omega^{2}} $$ (16.20a) implying that the equilibrium value, $ \tilde{d} $, satisfies $$ \widetilde{d}=\frac{\widetilde{d}}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\frac{[\sigma_{a}^{2}+\widetilde{d}]^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}+\widetilde{d}+\omega^{2}} $$

        "id": "formula_16.10a",
        "latex": "\\sigma_{z^{*}}^{2}=\\left(1-\\kappa\\right)\\sigma_{z}^{2}",

        "latex": "\\delta\\left(\\sigma_{z}^{2}\\right)=\\sigma_{z^{*}}^{2}-\\sigma_{z}^{2}=-\\kappa\\sigma_{z}^{2}",

      },

        "id": "formula_16.11a",

        "label": "Formula 16.11a",

      {
        "id": "formula_16.11b",
        "latex": "\\kappa=\\frac{2\\varphi\\left(x_{[1/2+p/2]}\\right)x_{[1/2+p/2]}}{p}",

        "section": "Short-term Changes in the Variance: 1. Changes in the Additive Variance: Introduction",

  "nearby_text": "e that assortative mating follows selection, and so the selected parental phenotypic values, $ z_f^* $ and $ z_{m^*} $, are correlated. Returning to Equation 16.5a, the offspring variance is given by $$ \\sigma^{2}(z_{o})=\\frac{h^{4}}{4}\\sigma^{2}\\left(z_{m}^{*}+z_{f}^{*}\\right)+\\sigma_{e}^{2} $$ (16.21a)"
}

label: Formula 16.21c
section: SELECTION WITH ASSORTATIVE MATING
subsection: Results Using the Infinitesimal Model
nearby_text: Assuming selection is such that $ \sigma^2(z_x^*) = (1 - \kappa_x)\sigma_z^2 $ for $ x = f $ or $ m $, Equation 16.21b becomes $$ \sigma^{2}(z_{o})=\frac{h^{4}}{2}\sigma_{z}^{2}\Biggl(1-\frac{\kappa_{f}+\kappa_{m}}{2}+\rho\sqrt{\left(1-\kappa_{f}\right)\left(1-\kappa_{m}\right)}\Biggr)+\sigma_{e}^{2} $$ (16.21c)

        "section": "CHANGES IN VARIANCE UNDER THE INFINITESIMAL MODEL",

label: Formula 16.22
section: SELECTION WITH ASSORTATIVE MATING
subsection: Results Using the Infinitesimal Model
nearby_text: LW Equations 7.18 through 7.20) follow immediately from Equations 16.12, 16.7c, and 16.13, respectively. More generally, when the amount of selection and assortative mating change in each generation, $$ d(t+1)=\frac{d(t)}{2}-\frac{\kappa(t)}{2}h^{2}(t)\sigma_{A}^{2}(t) $$ (16.22) where $ \kappa(t) $ is given by Equation 16.21d, with $ \kappa_{f}, \kappa_{m} $, and $ \rho $ taking on values for generation t.

  "nearby_text": "d > 0 $, reaching an upper limit (for these genetic and mating parameters) of $ \\widetilde{d} = 20.71 $. From Example 16.3, the single-generation response using a population with $ d(k) $ is given by $$ \\begin{aligned}R(k)=1.4h^{2}(k)\\sigma_{z}(k)&=1.4\\left(\\frac{\\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)+d(k)}{\\sigma_{z}^{2}(0)+d(k)}\\right)\\sqrt{\\sigma_{z}^{2}(0)+d(k)}\\\\&=1.4\\left(\\frac{50+d(k)}{100+d(k)}\\right)\\sqrt{100+d(k)}\\\\ \\end{aligned} $$ (16.23b)"
